findings.title: Passthrough column re-attach loop calls reset_index repeatedly on same frame
findings.why: Inside the loop over _passthrough_cols (line 388), input_for_model.reset_index(drop=True) is called on line 395 for EVERY column being re-attached. If N passthrough columns are present, the frame is reset N times, each creating a copy. On a 100GB frame, this is catastrophic (N Г— 100GB peak RAM). Should reset once before the loop.
findings.fix: Move reset_index call to line 387 (before the loop): `if _stashed_passthrough: input_for_model = input_for_model.reset_index(drop=True)`. Inside the loop, remove the reset and assign directly: `input_for_model[_pc] = _vals_aligned`.
findings.test: test_passthrough_cols_multiple_reattach: fit a model with 3+ passthrough columns on a polars frame; verify predict path creates only 1 reset, not N resets (monitor RAM peak or instrument reset_index call count).

findings.title: Passthrough column stashing converts single polars column to pandas per-column in tight loop
findings.why: Line 373 calls `_src_for_stash.get_column(_pc).to_pandas()` inside a loop over passthrough columns. Each column is converted to pandas independently, causing N separate to_pandas() calls instead of batching the select+convert. On a 100GB frame with even 2-3 passthrough columns, this serializes the conversion overhead and wastes peak memory.
findings.fix: Batch the column selection: `_selected = _src_for_stash.select(_passthrough_cols)` before the loop (line 370). Then inside the loop, either iterate over the polars result or convert once: `_stashed_pandas = _selected.to_pandas() if isinstance(_src_for_stash, pl.DataFrame) else _selected`. Iterate row-wise from the cached result.
findings.test: test_passthrough_cols_batch_conversion: fit a model with 3+ passthrough columns; verify only 1 select+to_pandas call occurs, not N separate calls (instrument get_column calls).

findings.title: Fallback pandas-view cache not used consistently in predict post-pipeline retry path
findings.file_line: core/_predict_pre_pipeline.py:188-220 (pandas_view_cache parameter exists but usage incomplete)
findings.why: The function signature accepts `pandas_view_cache: dict` as a parameter (line 167), intending to cache pandas views of polars frames across model retries. However, the stashing path (line 373) always calls fresh to_pandas() even when a cached view is available. This causes redundant conversions on polars inputs that trigger the fallback retry codepath.
findings.fix: Check the cache before calling to_pandas: `if isinstance(_src_for_stash, pl.DataFrame): _cache_key = id(_src_for_stash); if _cache_key not in pandas_view_cache: pandas_view_cache[_cache_key] = _src_for_stash.select(_passthrough_cols).to_pandas(); _selected = pandas_view_cache[_cache_key]`.
findings.test: test_predict_pandas_view_cache_reuse: trigger a predict that hits the fallback path multiple times (2+ models); verify the polars->pandas conversion is amortised (cache hit on 2nd retry).

findings.title: MTR OOF fold-refit loop materializes train rows multiple times via _slice_rows_by_idx
findings.why: For each of K folds, lines 100-101 call `_slice_rows_by_idx(X_train, tr_idx)` and similarly for y_train. On large frames (100k+ rows), each fold materializes a subset via iloc/filter. If the inner refits clone the data (sklearn.base.clone + fit), the subset is then cloned again inside fit, causing 2x peak materialization per fold. For K=5 and 100GB, this can exceed available RAM.
findings.fix: Use a view-deferred pattern: pass (X_train, tr_idx) tuples to the inner fit instead of materializing immediately, or use lazy/streaming iteration if the estimators support partial_fit. Alternatively, use a context manager that un-subsets after the fold completes.
findings.test: test_mtr_oof_memory_peak: fit MTR on a 50GB polars frame with kfold=5; log peak RSS during loop; verify it does not exceed 2Г—(50GB + KГ—subset_size).

findings.title: Single polars column to_pandas in predict passthrough path lacks per-column optimization
findings.why: When a single passthrough column is stashed from a polars frame, calling `get_column(_pc).to_pandas()` materializes just that column as a pandas Series. This is correct, but the resulting Series.reset_index() on line 375 for pandas input and repeated reset_index in the re-attach loop (line 395) compound the overhead. For many passthrough columns, the Series resets accumulate.
findings.fix: Cache the single column's pandas view and reuse it: `_series = _src_for_stash.get_column(_pc).to_pandas(); _stashed_passthrough[_pc] = _series`. In re-attach, avoid redundant reset: compute the reset offset once before the loop.
findings.test: test_passthrough_single_col_no_redundant_reset: stash and re-attach a single passthrough column; verify reset_index is called at most once on output_for_model, not per column.

findings.title: Neural MLP categorical factorization re-applies X.assign+select per eval_set tuple element
findings.why: Lines 126-131 call `X.assign(**encoded_cols)[ordered]` for the eval_set val frame. This pattern is safe (view-backed on numeric columns), but if eval_set is a list with multiple tuples (e.g., (val_df, y_val), (test_df, y_test)), the same pattern repeats. The actual code only processes the first eval tuple, but the pattern is fragile. More critically, on line 118, the initial `X.assign(**encoded_cols)[ordered]` creates a new frame even though only cat columns change.
findings.fix: For the initial X fit: instead of assign+select, mutate-and-restore using a context manager: keep numeric columns as-is, only replace cat columns. For eval_set: ensure eval_set is either None or a single tuple; batch-process all eval tuples in ONE pass if needed.
findings.test: test_mlp_cat_factorize_no_numeric_copy: train an MLP with categorical columns on a 5GB frame; verify the numeric columns stay as views (no deep copy); log memory peak.

findings.title: Arrow-backed pandas view optimization in fairness subgroup computation not used on all dataframe branches
findings.why: The fairness subgroup code correctly uses `get_pandas_view_of_polars_df()` (the Arrow split-blocks bridge) on line 458. However, the upstream `_phase_helpers_fit_split.py:541` has a fallback to bare `to_pandas()` when the Arrow view fails. This fallback catches exceptions silently and loses the 32x speedup on large frames. No warning is logged, so silent performance regressions can occur if the Arrow path ever fails.
findings.fix: Log a WARNING if the Arrow path fails, explaining the performance cost: `logger.warning('Arrow split-blocks pandas view failed (%s); falling back to .to_pandas() which is ~32x slower on large frames.', _exc)`. Also, investigate why the exception occurs and fix it upstream rather than falling back.
findings.test: test_fairness_arrow_view_failure_logging: mock get_pandas_view_of_polars_df to raise; verify a WARNING is logged with the performance explanation.

findings.title: Repeated reset_index() calls in predict passthrough reattach lack index awareness
findings.why: Lines 394-395 call reset_index(drop=True) on both _vals_aligned and input_for_model inside a loop. However, if the model's pre_pipeline already resets indices (common with sklearn pipelines), these resets are redundant. More critically, resetting the index on input_for_model INSIDE the loop means if a previous iteration's column assignment already reset it, the next iteration does it again (no-op but wasteful). The pattern suggests index management is not centralized.
findings.fix: Call reset_index once before the reattach loop: `input_for_model = input_for_model.reset_index(drop=True)` at line 387 (outside the loop). Inside the loop, ensure _vals_aligned is already aligned (computed before the loop). This is a pure refactor; behaviorally identical but eliminates redundant copies.
findings.test: test_predict_reset_index_single_call: instrument reset_index; verify it is called exactly once on input_for_model per predict call, not M times (M = number of passthrough columns).

findings.title: Polars select in error path materializes all requested columns even if not all are used
findings.why: Line 414 calls `input_for_model.select(_inner_list)` inside an exception handler (fallback path after pre_pipeline fails). The selection is correct but unconditional; if the error was not due to missing columns, the select is wasted work. Additionally, the list comprehension on line 410 `[str(c) for c in _inner_feat_names]` materializes the feature names twice (once for set comprehension, once for the condition check and select), wasting CPU on large feature counts (1000+).
findings.fix: Memoize the string-converted feature names: `_inner_list = [str(c) for c in _inner_feat_names] if _inner_feat_names else []; _have = {str(c) for c in input_for_model.columns}; if all(c in _have for c in _inner_list): input_for_model = input_for_model.select(_inner_list)`. Use _inner_list throughout to avoid recomputing.
findings.test: test_predict_error_path_feature_name_caching: verify [str(c) for c in ...] is evaluated once, not twice, in the error path (instrument with a counter or mock).

findings.title: Fallback pandas view cache key (id(polars_frame)) not collision-safe across function calls
findings.why: The pandas_view_cache is meant to deduplicate polars->pandas conversions across multiple model predict calls. Using `id()` as the cache key is unsafe because Python can reuse object IDs after garbage collection, especially if the input polars frame is re-created between calls. If two different polars frames are created and destroyed, then a third frame reuses the first's ID, the cache will incorrectly return a stale pandas view of the original data (silent correctness bug).
findings.fix: Use a content-based cache key: `_cache_key = (id(_src_for_stash), hash(tuple(_src_for_stash.columns)), _src_for_stash.shape)` or, better, replace the id-based cache with a WeakKeyDictionary: `from weakref import WeakKeyDictionary; pandas_view_cache = WeakKeyDictionary()` so the cache entry is automatically evicted when the polars frame is garbage-collected.
findings.test: test_pandas_view_cache_gc_safety: create and delete multiple polars frames; verify the cache does not return a stale view when a new frame reuses an old ID.

findings.title: Composite discovery pre-screen undershoots on collinear high-card columns due to missing polars_bytes materialization
findings.file_line: core/_phase_composite_discovery.py (not directly cited but per audit notes on pre-screen)
findings.why: While not explicitly in the current code, the CLAUDE.md audit notes (line 100-102) reference an unfixed bug where CatBoost + multilabel + inject_degenerate_cols masks a column-detection bug via canonicalisation. This suggests that somewhere in the composite discovery pre-screen path, when columns are injected or examined, the type-checking may not account for polars_bytes or other polars extensions, leading to silent feature loss on specific combo patterns.
findings.fix: Search for `_canonical_key:327-335` reference and implement the recommended `explicit cat_features= arg in CB wrapper (or type-cast guard) so CB doesn't auto-detect numeric columns as categorical when inject_degenerate_cols=True`.
findings.test: test_composite_discovery_inject_degenerate_with_multilabel_cb: run fuzz combo with CB + multilabel + inject_degenerate_cols=True; verify no silent column loss (regression test to pin the unfixed bug).

findings.title: Outlier detection val-collapse continues with unfiltered val, risking contaminated eval_set
findings.why: When OD rejects >99% of val rows, function logs ERROR but silently continues with ORIGINAL unfiltered val_df (line 244), returning None for val_od_idx (line 246). Downstream eval_set still uses this potentially contaminated val. Per CLAUDE.md rules (line 112-114), this is a layered 0-row val tolerance that 'points at upstream defect'. Should raise on severe collapse to force upstream fix instead of masking.
findings.fix: When val_kept < val_min_keep, raise ValueError instead of silent-continue. Only skip OD when pre-check determines OD would destroy minority class (which is already handled at line 216-229). This forces users to investigate OD contamination/fit-distribution mismatch immediately rather than discovering silent evaluation corruption later.
findings.test: Unit test: (1) synthetic 100-row val with OD fitted on train features that flag 95+ val as outliers, assert raises ValueError with 'val_min_keep' in message. (2) Existing fuzz suite: monitor fuzz results for eval_set metrics that spike when OD is combined with outlier-prone features (would catch silent leakage). Add regression sensor to `tests/training/test_fuzz_regression_sensors.py` pinning OD+multilabel+imbalance to a specific seed that previously triggered the collapse case.

findings.title: PartialFitESWrapper internal train/val split uses fixed random_state, breaking ES reproducibility and independence
findings.why: When external_X_val is None, `_split_train_val(X, y, self.val_size, self.random_state)` is called (line 190). The random_state is fixed at wrapper construction (line 142: `self.random_state = int(random_state)`), defaulting to 42 if not overridden. This causes: (a) deterministic-only behavior loss if the wrapper is instantiated multiple times with the same seed in the same process; (b) the internal split is NOT independent from the main train/val/test split, so ES val may not represent true holdout distribution; (c) if a user chains wrappers (PartialFitESWrapper wrapping another estimator), both use the same seed.
findings.fix: Use a seed derived from the main split seed (passed via fit_params or stored on wrapper) rather than a fixed constant. Or: require external_X_val/external_y_val when determinism is required, and allow random_state=None to trigger a fresh random seed per call. At minimum, add a docstring note that internal split is not independent from the main train/val split and users should supply external_X_val if reproducibility across wrapper instances matters.
findings.test: Unit test: (1) Create two wrappers with default random_state, fit on same X/y, verify they produce different ES val slices (they currently don't). (2) Fuzz: random-seed rotation on PartialFitESWrapper instantiation, monitor for determinism artifacts in ES curves. (3) Regression sensor: suite-level test that ES val distribution (mean/std of a continuous target) is NOT identical to main val distribution when wrapper is used.

findings.title: eval_set accepts 0-row val without explicit raise, trusting upstream OD guard that can silently continue
findings.why: Comment at line 600-607 states 'Historical 0-row val skip in _setup_eval_set removed 2026-04-28 (batch 4)'. The rationale: 'OD now guarded at source in _apply_outlier_detection_global (val-side min_keep floor + class-balance pre-check)'. However, _apply_outlier_detection_global's min_keep guard only applies when OD is enabled and val_kept < val_min_keep -- if OD is disabled or val_df is passed through unfiltered due to OD error (line 244), a 0-row val can still reach _setup_eval_set. The code now relies on CB raising 'Labels variable is empty' (line 606 comment) instead of failing fast upstream.
findings.fix: Add explicit guard at _setup_eval_set line 600 (inside each model_category branch): `if len(val_df) == 0: raise ValueError(f'val_df is empty; OD filtering or upstream error collapsed validation set. Investigate _apply_outlier_detection_global output.')`. This surfaces the root cause immediately instead of deferring to CB's opaque deep-C++ error.
findings.test: Unit test: directly call _setup_eval_set with val_df=pd.DataFrame() (empty), assert raises ValueError. Fuzz: with OD+heavy-contamination configs, monitor for either explicit ValueError (post-fix) or CB 'Labels empty' errors (pre-fix); post-fix should convert all to early ValueError in test logs.

findings.title: Group-aware + multilabel splitting silently falls back to GroupShuffleSplit, losing stratification guarantee without explicit failure
findings.why: When both group_ids and 2-D stratify_y (multilabel) are present, code tries to import MultilabelStratifiedGroupKFold (line 301-306). If import fails, it logs info-level message (line 308-314) and silently falls back to GroupShuffleSplit. This means multilabel-class proportions are NO LONGER preserved across train/val/test splits (line 312: 'multilabel proportions...not enforced'), but only an info log surfaces this change. A user may not realize stratification was abandoned, leading to silently-degenerate val splits with rare labels clustered in one split. Per CLAUDE.md (line 60-84), this is a 'defensive guard in production' masking an upstream problem: the right fix is to require iterative-stratification as a dependency OR fail fast when both conditions are met.
findings.fix: Change line 307-314 from log.info to raise: `if not _has_multilabel_iterstrat: raise ValueError(f'Group-aware splitting with multilabel targets requires iterative-stratification. Install: pip install iterative-stratification'). Alternatively: check at suite startup whether both use_groups AND multilabel target are present, and require iterative-stratification upfront.
findings.test: Unit test: (1) synthetic multilabel target + groups, iterative-stratification not installed, assert raises ValueError. (2) With iterative-stratification installed, verify stratification is actually enforced by checking multilabel-class ratios in train/val/test splits match the full distribution (within sampling noise). Regression sensor: fuzz with multilabel+groups combos, monitor for metric divergence between full-multilabel-stratified run vs fallback-GSS run; large divergence signals silent stratification loss.

findings.title: sample_weight may not propagate through CV inner-folds in RFECV/Composite discovery
findings.file_line: core/_phase_train_one_target_body.py:208-212 (marker setup), variable scoping in RFECV instantiation
findings.why: Line 99 captures sample_weights from ctx, and line 208 documents that 'only y / sample_weight differ inside the weight loop'. RFECV is initialized with marker `_mlframe_use_sample_weights_in_fs_` (line 99 in _setup_helpers_pre_pipelines.py), but RFECV's internal CV-fold construction (via sklearn's cross_val_score or StratifiedKFold) may not receive sample_weight for each fold. If the weight array is not explicitly passed through fit_params['sample_weight'] per fold, each inner CV train/val split resets to uniform weights, defeating weight-aware feature selection.
findings.fix: Audit RFECV.fit call signature in feature_selection/wrappers.py to verify sample_weight is passed as fit_params['sample_weight'] (or the equivalent per sklearn's cv-aware fit). If sample_weight array is indexed by full-dataset positions, ensure it's re-indexed per fold (e.g., sample_weight[train_idx] for each fold). Add a regression test that RFECV with sample_weight selects different features than uniform weights on an imbalanced synthetic dataset.
findings.test: Unit test: synthetic regression with 2 features (one signal, one noise) + highly imbalanced sample_weights (signal rows weight 1, noise rows weight 100), run RFECV with marker set, verify selected features are signal-dominant (would fail if weights ignored). Integration test: suite-level multilabel+weights+RFECV, compare feature selections with/without weight propagation; they should differ meaningfully.

findings.title: Pre-pipeline fit-transform ordering: custom pre-pipelines may fit on (train+val) if not guarded
findings.why: Comment at line 410-414 states 'no per-call reset, downstream `pre_pipeline.fit` may...'. Line 487 references pre_pipeline_name in a cache key, but the actual pre_pipeline.fit() call location is not immediately visible in this excerpt. However, the concern is real: if a custom pre-pipeline (e.g. MRMR, RFECV) is NOT reset between targets, its fit state may carry forward, fitting on contaminated data. More critically, if a custom transformer wraps sklearn's Pipeline and does not explicitly guard its fit, it may accidentally fit on val/test if those are passed in a data_loader or chained estimator context.
findings.fix: Ensure every pre_pipeline (MRMR, RFECV, custom) is cloned BEFORE fit (not reused), and fit is called ONLY on train_df, never on val_df/test_df. Add explicit assertion: after pre_pipeline.fit(), verify via hasattr check that fitted attributes are present only if expected (e.g. RFECV should have support_, XGBoost should have booster_). Document in pre_pipeline builder (line 21-49 in _setup_helpers_pre_pipelines.py) that custom pre-pipelines are cloned and fit train-only.
findings.test: Unit test: custom FeatureSelector that tracks which data it was fit on, insert into pre_pipelines list, run suite, assert it was fit on train_df shape only (never val/test). Regression sensor: fuzz with custom_pre_pipelines option enabled, monitor for NaN/inf in transformed val/test that would signal fit-time leakage (features fit on val would behave differently at test time).

findings.title: Pipeline transform state shared across targets, risking incomplete fit before val/test transform
findings.why: fit_and_transform_pipeline fits the polars-ds pipeline on train_df (line 108), then immediately transforms val_df (line 114) and test_df (line 120). If the pipeline state is lazily-initialized (e.g. caches fitted column stats), and pipeline.fit() returns before fully populating those caches, subsequent transform calls on val/test may hit uninitialized state. More concerning: if the same pipeline instance is passed across multiple targets (per-target optimization), fit on target-1's train may leave state that target-2's val tries to use before target-2's train has fit.
findings.fix: Ensure pipeline.fit(train_df) completes and all state is initialized before any transform() call. Add explicit check: `assert hasattr(pipeline, '_fitted_state')` or equivalent after fit, before any transform. Better: pipeline must be CLONED per target so targets never share fit state.
findings.test: Unit test: custom polars-ds pipeline that logs fit/transform calls with target_id, run suite with 2+ targets, verify fit(target-1-train) completes before transform(target-1-val) AND separate fit(target-2-train) occurs before transform(target-2-val). Regression sensor: fuzz with multi-target regression + polars input, monitor for val/test metrics that diverge unexpectedly between targets (would signal target-2 transform using target-1's fitted pipeline state).

findings.title: Multilabel eval_set carve extracts val target AFTER OD filter, risking shape mismatch with val_df
findings.why: In _train_one_target, current_val_target is extracted from targets dict at line 136-139. This extraction uses the original val_idx or val_df indices. However, if OD has filtered val_df to smaller shape (line 256 returns filtered_val_df + filtered_val_idx), the target extraction may use original indices that no longer align with the filtered val_df. For multilabel targets (2-D arrays), if val_df loses 50% of rows but current_val_target still has 100% (indexed wrong), eval_set construction (line 550+ in _data_helpers.py) receives misaligned (X: 50 rows, y: 100 rows) and eval_set setup crashes or silently trains on wrong (X,y) pairs.
findings.fix: After OD filter, re-extract current_val_target using filtered_val_idx (the indices that correspond to the filtered val_df), not the original indices. Assert len(current_val_target) == len(filtered_val_df) before passing to eval_set setup. In _setup_eval_set, add explicit shape check: `if len(val_df) != len(val_target): raise ValueError('val_df and val_target shape mismatch')`.
findings.test: Unit test: multilabel target + OD detector that removes >50% of val rows, verify eval_set is built with correct (filtered_val_df, filtered_val_target) shapes. Fuzz: multilabel + OD + early_stopping, assert eval_set shapes match in all paths. Regression sensor: monitor CB/LGB/XGB early-stopping metrics for NaN or sudden jumps when multilabel+OD are combined.

findings.title: Pipeline clone symmetry: cloned pre-pipelines may not be bit-identical if mutable state (caches, fitted attrs) exist
findings.why: At line 686 of _phase_train_one_target_body, comment says 'INTENTIONAL: clone() lives INSIDE the weight loop'. However, sklearn.base.clone() performs a shallow copy of fitted attributes (e.g. .coef_, .classes_), and if a pre-pipeline (e.g. MRMR) caches results in a module-level dict or class-level registry, the clone shares that cache. This means two cloned instances of the same MRMR will have different.get_params() but the same underlying cache, causing one target's cached fit to pollute another target's selection. Line 165-176 uses copy.deepcopy as fallback, but deepcopy is expensive (100+ GB frame case) and may duplicate large cached arrays.
findings.fix: For MRMR specifically: explicitly clear _FIT_CACHE (and any identity-cache override) after each target's fit, OR create a target-scoped cache dict that doesn't leak across targets. For general pre-pipelines: document that clone() creates independent instances for fit-time only; any per-call mutable state (caches, registries) must be reset/cleared between targets. Audit MRMR cache leakage: tests/training/test_fuzz_regression_sensors.py should verify target-1 MRMR selection в‰  target-2 MRMR selection when targets differ (currently may pass due to hidden cache reuse).
findings.test: Unit test: (1) MRMR on 2 targets with different distributions, assert selected features differ (would fail if cache is shared). (2) deepcopy fallback: 100GB polars frame wrapped in deepcopyable pre-pipeline, verify no OOM and selected features still differ per target. Regression sensor: multi-target fuzz with MRMR, monitor if per-target feature selections are suspiciously identical across unrelated targets (signal of cache leakage).

findings.title: Temporal split + group-aware split interaction not explicitly checked for incompatibility
findings.why: At line 145-176, timestamps are checked to route through temporal split. At line 288, group_ids are checked to route through group-aware split. However, there is no explicit check for the case where BOTH timestamps AND groups are present. Temporal split (TimeSeriesSplit or expanding-window) assumes chronological order; group-aware split (GroupShuffleSplit / StratifiedGroupKFold) assumes groups stay together. These two constraints may be incompatible on panel data (e.g. multiple time series, each a group). The code does not raise or document the interaction, risking silent mis-split where groups are broken by temporal ordering or temporal ordering is broken by group-keeping.
findings.fix: Add explicit check at line 286-288: `if timestamps is not None and _groups is not None: raise ValueError('Both timestamps and group_ids provided; temporal-aware group splitting not yet supported. Use either timestamps (TimeSeriesSplit) OR groups (GroupShuffleSplit), not both. For panel data, ensure groups correspond to time series IDs and use group-aware split.')`. OR implement proper temporal-group split (expanding-window per group).
findings.test: Unit test: synthetic panel data with 3 groups (time series), timestamps in chronological order, both passed to split, assert raises ValueError. For future: integration test with real panel data + temporal+group split, verify no leakage across groups and temporal order preserved per group.

findings.title: Imbalance resampling fit train-only not explicitly documented in trainer entry points, risking silent leakage if resampler fit scope expands
findings.file_line: core/_setup_helpers_pre_pipelines.py:1-49 (builder setup), no explicit resampler config/builder visible
findings.why: Per CLAUDE.md line 39-53 (Enable corrective mechanisms), any pre-pipeline that fits resampling (SMOTE, RandomUnderSampler, etc.) MUST fit train-only and apply train-only. However, in the _setup_helpers_pre_pipelines.py builder, there is no explicit section that handles imbalance-resampling pre-pipelines (only MRMR, RFECV, BorutaShap are visible). If imbalance config exists elsewhere in the suite and builds a resampler, the fit scope may not be explicitly documented, leaving future developers to guess whether resampler is fit train-only or accidentally fit on train+val during cross-val.
findings.fix: If imbalance resampling is used, add explicit section to _setup_helpers_pre_pipelines.py (lines 159+) that documents: 'Imbalance resampling (if enabled): resampler fits TRAIN ONLY, no fitting on val during cross-val'. Add a marker attribute like `setattr(resampler, '_mlframe_fit_train_only_', True)` so downstream code can verify this invariant. Alternatively, move imbalance resampling OUT of pre_pipelines and into the main training pipeline after pre_pipeline fit completes, so the scope is unambiguous.
findings.test: Unit test: if imbalance resampling is in scope, verify it's NOT fit on val during any nested CV. Fuzz: imbalance_cfg + CV models (RFECV, cross-val score), monitor for metric drift that would signal resampler leak into val. Regression sensor: synthetic imbalanced data, RFECV CV-fold, verify selected features are stable across folds (would diverge if resampler incorrectly fit per fold).

findings.title: Val-side outlier detection doesn't raise when min_keep violated; only logs
findings.why: Train-side raises on <1% survivors (line 169); val-side silently continues with original unfiltered val_df. Masked bug: downstream eval_set / early-stopping paths may receive 0-row or <1% val, causing downstream crashes or silent eval failure. Real root cause (misconfigured outlier detector) stays hidden.
findings.fix: Change line 244-246 to raise ValueError like train-side (line 169-174), or at minimum log.error + set val_df=None to fail-fast downstream instead of silently training without validation.
findings.test: Regression test: outlier_detection with aggressive contamination on val-skewed synthetic data. Assert that either (a) ValueError raises with 'rejected X of Y val' message, or (b) val_df becomes None. Verify downstream early-stopping code handles None val gracefully.

findings.title: CB cat_features may receive numeric columns when inject_degenerate_cols=True
findings.why: The combo.py canon rule for CB+multilabel+degenerate was retired (comment line 825), claiming a fix via 'explicit cat_features list' + 'num-degenerate guard'. However, _training_loop.py lines 338-414 only handle category-dtype columns. When inject_degenerate_cols=True creates num_const and num_null (numeric dtypes), CB's auto-detect may still mis-classify them as categorical on the polars-native path, causing TypeError or silent misfit.
findings.fix: Add a pre-fit guard in _training_loop.py after line 352 (_explicit_cats = set(...)) to filter cat_features to only columns whose original dtype is categorical (or explicitly user-specified); drop numeric-dtype columns that somehow landed in cat_features. Mirror the dtype check from _filter_polars_cat_features_by_dtype (misc_helpers.py:477) for the pandas path.
findings.test: Regression test: CB+multilabel+inject_degenerate_cols=True with small n (to force the degenerate injection). Assert no TypeError from CB Pool builder about 'No matching signature'. Verify num_const/num_null are excluded from final fit_params['cat_features'].

findings.title: Val-side outlier detection min_keep logs error but returns unfiltered original val_df
findings.why: The comment (line 232-233) says 'downstream eval paths handle empty val poorly', so the code returns the original val instead of raising. But this is a masked-bug pattern from CLAUDE.md: 'defensive guards in production should only skip when the empty path is a legitimate user scenario'. Here, a near-empty val is NOT legitimate - it signals a misconfigured detector or train/val distribution mismatch. Silently continuing trains a model you cannot properly evaluate.
findings.fix: Either: (a) raise ValueError like train-side (high safety), or (b) set val_df=None + val_idx=None + val_od_idx=None, which will cause downstream pipeline code (pipeline_fit_transform.py:245) to set val=None gracefully. Option (b) is safer if downstream code legitimately handles None val.
findings.test: Fuzz combo: outlier_detection + val_sequential_fraction >= 0.5 + aging_limit that could trim val. Verify the combo either raises or silently degrades to no-val training (not silent silent training with corrupted val).

findings.title: No explicit guard preventing numeric-dtype cat_features on CB degenerate+multilabel path
findings.why: The inject_degenerate_cols canon was retired claiming a 'num-degenerate guard', but the actual code doesn't explicitly drop numeric columns from cat_features before CB.fit. CLAUDE.md line 828 mentions 'feature_selection/wrappers.py' dtype-aware filter, but that's for RFECV inner-CV, not the main train path. The _training_loop.py fix (lines 338-414) handles category-dtype narrowing, not numeric-column exclusion.
findings.fix: Add a new filter in _training_loop.py after the category-dtype widen/narrow logic (after line 414): for pandas cat_features, drop any column whose dtype is numeric (int/float). For polars, use _filter_polars_cat_features_by_dtype (already there at fit-time via _misc_helpers.py). Explicit list is safer than relying on CB's auto-detect.
findings.test: Unit test: _train_model_with_fallback with inject_degenerate_cols=True + CB + small n_rows. Assert fit_params['cat_features'] contains ONLY categorical/string columns after the guard runs. Regression test: the original fuzz combo that triggered c0062 (CB+multilabel+degenerate) should still pass.

findings.title: RobustScaler zero-IQR guard exists but depends on explicit _select_scalable_numeric_columns call
findings.why: The fix (line 646) calls _select_scalable_numeric_columns before polars-ds.robust_scale. However, this is NOT a production-wide guard - it's specific to the polars-ds pipeline branch. sklearn RobustScaler in the sklearn-bridge path (line 510 'RobustScaler()') has no such guard. If zero-IQR columns reach sklearn RobustScaler, it will silently scale to NaN/inf. CLAUDE.md c0008/c0116 are about polars-ds, but the bug class is broader.
findings.fix: Add the same _select_scalable_numeric_columns filter to sklearn RobustScaler instantiation in the sklearn-bridge path. Wrap fit/fit_transform to filter zero-spread columns before delegating to sklearn. Document the rationale (zero-IQR -> NaN scaling -> downstream NaN handling issues).
findings.test: Regression test: zero-IQR column in the sklearn-bridge path (prefer_polarsds=False + scaler_name='robust' + inject_degenerate_cols=True). Assert no NaN-in-scaled columns reaching downstream. Verify the guard is symmetric across polars-ds and sklearn paths.

findings.title: Outlier detection train-side has min_keep guard, val-side only has tolerance floor (inconsistent failure modes)
findings.why: Train-side min_keep guard is asymmetric: train RAISES, val only TOLERATES. This violates CLAUDE.md principle 'Every bug detector (like fuzz tests) must be reflected in prod code'. The asymmetry hides a class of bugs: outlier detectors that destroy the minority class. Train-side min_keep prevents total-class collapse; val-side should do the same - if val collapses to <1%, either raise or signal early-stopping to use only train metrics.
findings.fix: Promote val-side from logger.error+continue to raise (matching train) or to return (val_df=None, val_idx=None) to trigger downstream no-val path. Document why: 'if OD collapses val to <1%, training is invalid without separate test evaluation'.
findings.test: Fuzz combo assertion: if train OD survivors >= min_keep but val OD survivors < min_keep, verify the mode is either (a) ValueError raised, or (b) val_df becomes None (no silent silent val eval). Measure: 0 combos should reach downstream trainer with mysteriously-small val after OD.

findings.title: Polars-nullable categorical columns may roundtrip with category-list disagreement
findings.why: The comment at line 457-461 mentions a flake where polars_nullable->pandas conversion leaves category-dtype with disagreeing categories between train and val/test. The fix (lines 471-487) aligns categories right before fit. However, this is a band-aid - the real bug is upstream in the conversion step. If conversion is lossy, this guard will continue to fail on new category orderings introduced in later data splits.
findings.fix: Root cause: audit where polars_nullable->pandas conversion happens (likely in core.py lazy-conversion). Ensure conversion either (a) preserves category order consistently, or (b) uses unordered categories + explicitly manages the codebook. The _align_xgb_cat_categories guard is temporary; the conversion should be category-safe at the source.
findings.test: Regression test: polars_nullable input with categorical columns + test_size < val_size (so test sees unseen categories). Assert _align_xgb_cat_categories does NOT need to fix mismatches, OR document why the flake is unavoidable and measure: category-rebuild overhead should be <1% of total fit time.

findings.title: Empty validation set from outlier detection + aging/sequential split not explicitly raised upstream
findings.why: CLAUDE.md notes four layered 0-row val tolerances. Two have been removed (_data_helpers.py:600). But the root cause (outlier detection + split edge case collapsing val) only has a 1% tolerance floor that logs, not raises. The combo.py comment (lines 929-935) mentions 'zero-row variant' reaching SimpleImputer. The guard at line 935-944 is a canonicalization (dedup), not a fix.
findings.fix: Add an explicit check in core._apply_outlier_detection_global after val filtering: if val is not None and val_kept == 0, raise ValueError immediately. This matches train-side behavior and prevents downstream surprises.
findings.test: Regression test: outlier_detection=isolation_forest + trainset_aging_limit or val_sequential_fraction=1.0 + backward placement on small n_rows. Assert: either (a) ValueError from OD, or (b) val_df becomes None before pipeline (no 0-row val reaches pipeline).

findings.title: CB text_processing dynamic calibration is fit-time, not RFECV-inner-fold aware
findings.why: The CLAUDE.md comment (lines 799-801) says the text_processing fix is called from 'trainer._train_model_with_fallback AND the RFECV inner-fold path'. The fit-time path is at _training_loop.py:434. But is the RFECV path actually calling it? If RFECV inner folds don't apply the same dynamic OLB calibration, small folds could still hang.
findings.fix: Verify feature_selection/wrappers.py RFECV inner-fold calls compute_cb_text_processing before each inner CB fit. If not, add it. Or hoist the calibration to a pre-fit wrapper so all CB fits (including RFECV inner) use it automatically.
findings.test: Regression test: RFECV+CB+text_features+small n_rows. Measure: no hangs on inner folds. Verify log shows 'scaled CB text_processing' at least once per RFECV fit, not just the outer fit.

findings.title: Silent return(None,None) on dtype error masks upstream validation gap
findings.why: When model.fit() raises 'pandas dtypes must be int, float or bool', _train_model_with_fallback logs a warning and returns (None, None). Downstream callers expecting a fitted model receive silence; the suite continues with None as the model and silently produces NaN metrics. This hides a real upstream bug: the dtype check should have rejected the data BEFORE reaching fit().
findings.fix: Replace with a raised ValueError that names the offending column(s) and dtype(s). Let the error propagate to _handle_training_error / _train_model_with_fallback's outer exception handler so the suite logs the failure per-model instead of per-fit-retry attempt. Rationale: dtype errors are NOT transient (unlike OOM / callback availability); retrying is futile and silence deceives users about what went wrong.
findings.test: Add regression test in test_fuzz_regression_sensors.py: train on synthetic frame with a polars-ds-transformed string column that slips past preprocessing and reaches CB/LGB fit. Assert that the error surfaces in the suite report with the column name, not as silent (None, None) downstream metrics.

findings.title: Bare 'except Exception' in cached pipeline state transfer conceals copy failures
findings.why: When copying fitted state from cache (lines 625-627), each attribute catch-all swallows copy() failures silently (line 628: 'except Exception: pass') and falls back to the original reference. A non-copyable fitted attribute (e.g. a lambda, file handle, or sklearn internal memo dict) then gets shared between the cache and the current pipeline. Downstream mutations corrupt the cache silently. CLAUDE.md rule В§ 'Silently-swallowed exceptions bare except returning None': this is a template violation.
findings.fix: Tighten the except to catch only TypeError / pickle.PicklingError (the known copy() failure modes for non-serializable attrs). Re-raise any other exception so misconfigured transformers surface loudly. Add a unit test that verifies the cache-hit path with a deliberately non-copyable custom transformer and asserts that either (a) the copy succeeds cleanly or (b) an exception surfaced and the cache path was skipped.
findings.test: test_pipeline_cache_state_transfer_handles_noncopyable_attrs: fit a custom transformer with a __dict__[non_copyable] = lambda: 1, cache it, hit the cache, verify the current pipeline has an independent copy (not shared reference). Pre-fix: lambda gets shared, post-fix: either copied cleanly or exception logged.

findings.title: Silent skip of category alignment via bare 'except Exception' on dtype access hides corrupted categorical columns
findings.why: When scanning train_df for Categorical columns to align across train/val/test (lines 101-117), a bare 'except Exception' on dtype access (line 104) silently skips the column and logs at DEBUG (line 110-114). If the column's dtype access raises due to a polars schema error / corrupted CategoricalDtype / custom dtype wrapper, that column never gets added to cat_cols_to_align and XGBoost later crashes with 'unseen category'. The exception could signal a real data integrity issue (polars enum/cat dtype mismatch) but gets buried.
findings.fix: Narrow the except to catch only TypeError / AttributeError (legitimate cases where a column has no .dtype attr or dtype is not a Categorical). Re-raise any other exception (ValueError / KeyError from polars schema access). This surfaces real data issues early instead of masking them as downstream XGB 'unseen category' crashes.
findings.test: test_eval_cat_alignment_raises_on_corrupted_categorical_dtype: create a pandas DataFrame with a column whose .dtype getter raises ValueError (custom descriptor), call _align_xgb_cat_categories, assert that the error surfaces (not silently skipped). Verify that the error message includes the column name.

findings.title: Missing validation for 0-row train after outlier detection: silent min_keep floor instead of early error
findings.why: When outlier detection rejects >99% of training rows, _apply_outlier_detection_global raises ValueError with min_keep (lines 167-174). However, this error is only logged at the SOURCE; if a downstream trainer catches and swallows it (e.g. broad except in the CV loop), training continues with an effectively empty dataset. The error message does not name which outlier detector caused this, making it hard to debug which config combination is misconfigured. CLAUDE.md example: 'four layered 0-row val tolerances in trainer.py.. all point at the same upstream defect'.
findings.fix: Add the outlier detector CLASS NAME and (if available) its contamination / n_neighbors / threshold parameters to the error message. Raise a custom OutlierDetectionError subclass instead of ValueError so callers can catch and handle it distinctly from data errors. Ensure no except-all in train/val split or CV loop swallows this error.
findings.test: test_outlier_detection_error_message_includes_detector_name: fit with contamination=0.99 on a synthetic frame with a strong correlated feature, verify that the raised error mentions the detector class and contamination parameter.

findings.title: Unclear error message when NaN-bearing data reaches outlier detector without imputer wrapper
findings.why: When outlier detector.fit() raises ValueError due to NaN (line 92), the error log (lines 96-102) suggests wrapping the detector in Pipeline([SimpleImputer(), detector]). However, the message doesn't explain WHEN to apply this: before passing to the suite config, or is there a default imputation step that should have run? Users see 'wrap in Pipeline' but don't know if that's a user responsibility or an mlframe config gap. This is a UX clarity issue that CLAUDE.md 'error messages should be clearer' addresses.
findings.fix: Enhance the error message to include: (1) which column(s) contain NaN (if accessible), (2) that OutlierDetectionConfig accepts a pre-fitted pipeline if the user wants custom imputation, and (3) the outlier detection runs BEFORE the main preprocessing pipeline so SimpleImputer is not auto-applied. Add a reference to the suite documentation or an example config showing how to wrap.
findings.test: test_outlier_detector_nan_error_message_clarity: call _apply_outlier_detection_global with LocalOutlierFactor on NaN-bearing data, capture the error log, verify it names the columns and mentions OutlierDetectionConfig.

findings.title: Bare 'except Exception' on class-balance pre-check masks real validation failures silently
findings.why: Two locations where class-balance pre-check (verifying OD won't destroy minority classes) catches 'except Exception' and logs at DEBUG (lines 154-155, 230-231). If the target array indexing / shape checking raises due to a real bug (mismatched indices, target is None when expected), the exception is silently swallowed and the check is skipped. Downstream, OD may then destroy the minority class without the safety net, leading to cryptic CB 'only one class remains' errors far from the real cause.
findings.fix: Narrow both except blocks to catch only IndexError / TypeError (legitimate indexing failures on None/empty targets). Re-raise other exceptions (ValueError / AttributeError from target schema issues) so they surface immediately as configuration errors rather than latent class-destruction bugs.
findings.test: test_class_balance_precheck_raises_on_mismatched_target_shape: call _apply_outlier_detection_global with targets_for_classbalance={'target': np.array([1,0])} but train_idx pointing beyond array bounds, verify the exception surfaces (not silently debugged).

findings.title: No validation that pre_pipeline output columns match model's expected feature count
findings.why: When _apply_pre_pipeline_transforms fits a pre_pipeline (feature selector + preprocessing), it returns train_df_cached / val_df_cached with potentially fewer columns (feature selector dropped columns). The returned shape is never validated against model expectations. If a feature selector has a bug and returns 0 columns, or if the pipeline accidentally drops ALL features, the next model.fit() receives an empty DataFrame and crashes with an opaque 'X is empty' or 'no features' error with no context that the selector was at fault.
findings.fix: After pre_pipeline.fit_transform returns (line 700+), validate that the output DataFrame has >=1 column. If the selector returned 0 columns, raise ValueError('Feature selector dropped all columns: ...') with the selector class name and input/output shapes. This surfaces the bug at the pipeline layer instead of in the model layer.
findings.test: test_pre_pipeline_validates_output_column_count: create a custom selector that always returns 0 features, run _apply_pre_pipeline_transforms with it, assert that the error mentions '0 columns' and names the selector.

findings.title: Missing assertion that val_df passed to _setup_eval_set is not empty after outlier filtering
findings.why: CLAUDE.md notes: 'four layered 0-row val tolerances in trainer.py (in _apply_pre_pipeline_transforms Г—2, _setup_eval_set, _compute_split_metrics). All point at the same upstream defect: outlier detection / aging-limit collapsing val to 0 rows silently.' Line 600-607 explicitly removed the historical 0-row val skip with a comment 'let CB raise its own Labels variable is empty'. However, if a 0-row val IS passed and CB raises, the error is cryptic ('Labels variable is empty') and does not indicate that the outlier detector destroyed the validation set. The upstream guard (core._apply_outlier_detection_global at line 234-246) logs an error but CONTINUES with the ORIGINAL val_df, which is a workaround, not a fix.
findings.fix: Raise an explicit guard in _setup_eval_set: if len(val_df) == 0 (checked at entry, line 545), raise ValueError('Validation set is empty. This typically means outlier detection or data aging filters removed all rows from val. Check OutlierDetectionConfig and TrainingSplitConfig settings.'). Move this guard inline where the old skip was (line 600-607). Rationale: an empty val is a configuration issue, not a data issue; fail fast so users see the real cause.
findings.test: test_setup_eval_set_raises_on_empty_val: call _setup_eval_set with an empty val_df, assert that it raises ValueError with a message mentioning outlier detection config.
